title: Incident Debugging & Rollbacks
description: Using Git for production incident response is a critical Staff+ skill. Understanding how to leverage git bisect, the reflog, and automated rollback pipelines can mean the difference between minutes and hours of downtime. This guide covers forensic Git techniques and automation patterns used at FAANG scale.

sections.title: Git Bisect Deep Dive
sections.description:
Git bisect performs a binary search through your commit history to find the exact commit that introduced a bug. At FAANG scale, bisect is automated and wired into CI pipelines to catch regressions in minutes rather than hours.

How it works:

Bisect maintains a binary search over the commit DAG. Given N commits between good and bad, it performs O(log N) checkouts. At each step, it marks the current commit as good or bad, halving the remaining search space.
sections.codeBlock:
# Manual bisect session
git bisect start
git bisect bad HEAD
git bisect good v1.0.0
# Git checks out a commit halfway between good and bad
# Test the current state and mark:
# git bisect good  # if no bug
# git bisect bad   # if bug present

# Automated bisect with script:
git bisect run npm test

# Bisect with skip for untestable commits:
git bisect skip

# View bisect log:
git bisect log

sections.title: Reflog Forensics
sections.description:
The reflog records every movement of HEAD — including resets, rebases, and amend operations. This is your forensic trail when commits seem to have "disappeared." At FAANG, reflog analysis is the first step in any post-incident investigation.

The reflog has two namespaces: HEAD (all HEAD movements) and refs/heads/<branch> (branch-specific). Reflog entries expire after 90 days by default (gc.reflogExpire).
sections.codeBlock:
# View reflog for HEAD
git reflog
# 1234567 HEAD@{0}: commit: fix: resolve null pointer
# abcdef0 HEAD@{1}: reset: moving to HEAD~3
# 89abcde HEAD@{2}: rebase: checkout feature-branch

# View reflog for a specific branch:
git reflog show main

# Recover a "lost" commit after a bad reset:
git reset --hard HEAD@{2}

# Or cherry-pick it:
git cherry-pick 1234567

# Export reflog for analysis:
git reflog --date=iso > reflog-export.txt

# Set longer reflog retention for critical repos:
git config gc.reflogExpire never
git config gc.reflogExpireUnreachable 90.days

sections.title: Automated Rollbacks
sections.description:
At FAANG scale, rollbacks must be automated, tested, and deployable within minutes. A robust rollback pipeline follows these stages:

  1. Detection: Monitoring alerts trigger the pipeline (error budget burn, latency spikes, 5xx surges)
  2. Identification: git bisect or deploy tag pinpoints the bad change
  3. Revert: Automated revert commit is generated and applied
  4. Verification: Tests run against the reverted state
  5. Deploy: Rolled out with gradual traffic shift
  6. Postmortem: Issue created with bisect results, revert commit, and timeline
sections.codeBlock:
# Example: Automated rollback workflow (partial)
# Step 1: Find the bad deploy tag
git tag --sort=-creatordate | head -5

# Step 2: Bisect between last known good and current
git bisect start HEAD v1.0.0

# Step 3: Run the test suite automatically
git bisect run ./ci/run-tests.sh

# Step 4: Create revert commit
git revert --no-edit $(git bisect bad --no-checkout)

# Step 5: Create the rollback tag
git tag -a "rollback-$(date +%s)" -m "Automated rollback"

# Step 6: Push and trigger deploy
git push origin main --force-with-lease

sections.title: Incident Runbooks as Git Workflows
sections.description:
On-call runbooks should live in the repository as markdown files with executable checklists. GitHub Issues can track incident response with automated triggers via Actions.

Key patterns for incident runbooks:

  • Runbook as Code: Runbooks live at .github/runbooks/<scenario>.md
  • Issue Templates: Incident report templates with severity classification
  • Action Triggers: Issue labeled incident triggers automated diagnostic workflows
  • Bisect Automation: One-click workflow to start a bisect against production deploy tags
sections.codeBlock:
# .github/runbooks/high-latency.md
# High Latency Incident Runbook

## Severity Assessment
- P0: > 5s p99 latency for 5+ minutes
- P1: > 2s p99 latency for 15+ minutes
- P2: > 500ms p99 latency

## Diagnostics
1. [ ] Run: gh issue create --label incident --title "Latency spike $(date)"
2. [ ] Run: git bisect start production-deploy-1 production-deploy-0
3. [ ] Run: kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu > /tmp/pod-stats
4. [ ] Run: gh api /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/workflows > /tmp/active-workflows

## Resolution
1. [ ] Identify bad commit via bisect
2. [ ] git revert <bad-commit>
3. [ ] Deploy revert through canary
4. [ ] Monitor for 15 minutes
5. [ ] Close incident issue with postmortem

details:
Incident debugging at FAANG scale is a discipline, not a skill. The most important patterns are:

  • Automate the bisect: Wire git bisect into your CI so every deploy tag is recoverable. Google's internal tooling can auto-bisect across thousands of changelists in seconds using distributed checkpoint comparison.
  • Reflog as audit trail: Train your SRE team to read the reflog before asking "who did what." The reflog is append-only and cannot be tampered with locally.
  • Deploy tags are truth: Every production deployment should create a signed, annotated tag. Rollback becomes a simple tag navigation and revert.
  • Rollback posture: Optimize for rollback speed, not hotfix speed. A hotfix that takes 30 minutes is worse than a rollback that takes 3 minutes. Your team should be faster at rolling back than at identifying root cause.
  • Postmortem automation: Automatically create postmortem issues with bisect results, revert commit links, deploy tags, and timeline data. This reduces the friction of writing postmortems and ensures consistency.
